data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Old National Bancorp (ONB) After Mixed Share Price Performance?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Old National Bancorp at around US$22.85 is offering fair value or a potential opportunity, it helps to step back and look at what the recent share performance is really telling you.
  • The stock price return data is mixed, with a 0.8% decline over the past 7 days and a 0.6% decline over 30 days, set against gains of 1.3% year to date, 3.0% over 1 year, 41.0% over 3 years, and 48.2% over 5 years.

For Old National Bancorp, that context is important because it can influence how the market reacts to new information, even when company specific headlines are relatively muted.
  • On our valuation checks, Old National Bancorp scores 3 out of 6, as shown in its valuation score. This suggests that some measures point to potential undervaluation while others do not. Approach 1: Old National Bancorp Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank is expected to earn on its equity above the return that shareholders require, then capitalizes those surplus earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Old National Bancorp, the starting point is an estimated Book Value of $20.67 per share and a Stable EPS of $2.85 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 8 analysts. The model applies a Cost of Equity of $1.84 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $1.02 per share. In other words, the bank is expected to earn more on its equity than the return investors are assumed to require.

The Average Return on Equity is set at 11.70%, and analysts see Stable Book Value at $24.39 per share, based on forecasts from 10 analysts. Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns framework arrives at an intrinsic value of about $48.13 per share. Against a recent share price around $22.85, this implies the stock is treated as 52.5% undervalued under this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Old National Bancorp Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like Old National Bancorp, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. It gives you a quick feel for how the market is pricing the company’s earnings power today.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how investors view growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one.

Old National Bancorp currently trades on a P/E of 15.12x. That is above the Banks industry average of 11.88x and also above a peer group average of 13.60x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Old National Bancorp is 18.55x, which is its proprietary view of what the P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, size and key risks.

Because the Fair Ratio blends these company specific drivers, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, which often ignores differences in quality, risk and growth profiles. With a Fair Ratio of 18.55x versus the current 15.12x, Old National Bancorp appears modestly undervalued on this measure.

Approach 1: Visteon Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in your hand right now.

For Visteon, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $373.9 million, and analysts provide explicit forecasts out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extrapolating cash flows beyond that to build a 10 year path. For example, projected free cash flow in 2026 is $224.3 million and in 2027 is $229.8 million, with later years tapering to around $160.1 million by 2035, all in dollar terms.

When all of those projected cash flows are discounted back, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at about $94.62 per share. Against the recent share price of US$98.22, the DCF implies Visteon is roughly 3.8% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

In simple terms, a higher P/E usually means investors are willing to pay more for each dollar of earnings, often because they expect stronger growth or see the earnings as relatively dependable.

What counts as a normal P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings look. Higher growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to support a lower one.
